coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodot-decorated alveolate N, O, S tridoped hierarchical porous carbon as efficient electrocatalysis of polysulfide conversion for lithium-sulfur batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Mei-e
Guan, Jindiao
Sun, Jingchun
Guo, Hui
Xiao, Zhubing
Zhou, Nan
Gui, Qingwen
Gong, Daoxin
coredata.dc:description: Rechargeable lithium-sulfur (Li−S) batteries are a significant energy-storage device owing to their eco-friendliness and high theoretical energy density. However, the shuttle effect of soluble polysulfides as well as the slow redox kinetics constrains the development of Li-S batteries. Herein, carbon nanodots-decorated alveolate N, O, S tridoped hierarchical porous carbon (a-NOSPC) material is synthesized via facile pyrolysis of tobacco stem. The coexistence of micropores, mesopores, and macropore in the hierarchical porous carbon are beneficial for physical accommodating/immobilizing active materials sulfur and rapid charge/ion transfer. Meanwhile, the N, O, S dopants provide rich electrocatalytic active site in chemical binding of polysulfides and their accelerated redox kinetics, thus guaranteeing high utilization on active materials. The resultant a-NOSPC/S cathode with an 2.7 mg cm−2 areal sulfur loading delivers a high reversible areal capacity of 2.90 mAh cm−2 at 0.1 C, maintaining 2.01 mAh cm−2 over 100 cycles, which is obviously superior to the most reported biochar-based electrodes. Our results provide an appealing avenue to the design of multifunctional sulfur host for advanced Li-S batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-sulfur batteries
Tobacco stem
Nanodots-decorated
Alveolate
Tridoped

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of tobacco stem a-NOSPC biochar

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration the synthesis method of (I) a-NOSPC and (II) a-NOSPC/S. (III) charge/discharge process of a-NOSPC/S, (IV) Schematic illustration of the interface interactions in a-NOSPC/S electrode. The N, O, P dopants in the carbon matrixes act as the active anchor sites to entrap the long-chain polysulfides and localize them on the surface of electrode to facilitate electrochemical catalysis.
The (a,b) SEM, (c–f) TEM images, (g) the selected area electron diffraction image from f, and (h–l) the EDS mapping of a-NOSPC.

(a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman spectrum, (c) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, (d) pore size distribution, (e) XPS survey, High-resolution XPS spectra of (f) O 1s, (g) C 1s, (h) N 1s, and (i) S 2p of a-NOSPC.
(a) SEM image, (b) STEM, (c) EDS elemental mapping, (d) TEM image of a-NOSPC/S composite. (e) XRD patterns of S and a-NOSPC/S, (f) TG plots of S, a-NOSPC, and a-NOSPC/S.

(a) The first five cycles of CV curves of the a-NOSPC/S electrode. (b) Rate capability of the a-NOSPC/S electrode at various current rates. (c) Typical galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of the a-NOSPC/S cathode at current rates of 0.05–3 C. (d) Cycling capability of the a-NOSPC/S electrode at a current rate of 0.5 C with ASL of 1.1 mg cm−2. e) Nyquist curves from EIS measurements for the cell after different cycles. The inset in e is the equivalent circuit. The Rct is the charge-transfer resistance and Wo is the Warburg impendence.

UV–vis spectra of the Li2S4 solution with a-NOSPC (inset photographs are the blank Li2S4 solution and Li2S4 solution with a-NOSPC). High-resolution (b) Li 1s and (c) S2p XPS spectra of Li2S4 and a-NOSPC-Li2S4. (d) Polarization curves of symmetrical Li2S6-Li2S6 cell. (e) SEM and (f) TEM images of a-NOSPC/S composites after 400 cycles at discharge state. The scale bar in the inset of (f) is 30 nm.

Cycling performances of a-NOSPC/S electrode at 0.2 C with ASL of 2.7 mg cm−2. (b) The 10th cycle of galvanostatic charge/discharge profile of a-NOSPC/S electrode at 0.2 C. (c) Comparison of areal sulfur loading vs areal capacity with our reports and some recent biochar-based publications on Li-S batteries from Ref. [17, 20, 23–28, 50–53]. (d) Digital photographs of 60 green indicators composed of 2835-type LED modules propelled by four a-NOSPC/S batteries in series. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodot-decorated alveolate N, O, S tridoped hierarchical porous carbon as efficient electrocatalysis of polysulfide conversion for lithium-sulfur batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha, 410128, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry, Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Fuzhou, 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Structural Chemistry
Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha, 410128, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rechargeable lithium-sulfur (Li−S) batteries are a significant energy-storage device owing to their eco-friendliness and high theoretical energy density. However, the shuttle effect of soluble polysulfides as well as the slow redox kinetics constrains the development of Li-S batteries. Herein, carbon nanodots-decorated alveolate N, O, S tridoped hierarchical porous carbon (a-NOSPC) material is synthesized via facile pyrolysis of tobacco stem. The coexistence of micropores, mesopores, and macropore in the hierarchical porous carbon are beneficial for physical accommodating/immobilizing active materials sulfur and rapid charge/ion transfer. Meanwhile, the N, O, S dopants provide rich electrocatalytic active site in chemical binding of polysulfides and their accelerated redox kinetics, thus guaranteeing high utilization on active materials. The resultant a-NOSPC/S cathode with an 2.7 mg cm−2 areal sulfur loading delivers a high reversible areal capacity of 2.90 mAh cm−2 at 0.1 C, maintaining 2.01 mAh cm−2 over 100 cycles, which is obviously superior to the most reported biochar-based electrodes. Our results provide an appealing avenue to the design of multifunctional sulfur host for advanced Li-S batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Developing new battery systems with superior energy/power density, long lifespan and low cost has been of much significance owing to ever-growing demands for plug-in electric vehicle and large-scale energy storage devices. Lithium-sulfur (Li-S) batteries have been given substantial attention in the powerful energy storage technologies because of their high energy density, together with the non-toxicity, low cost and high abundance of elemental sulfur [1–4]. Nevertheless, the industrialization of Li-S batteries still remains impeded by three main challenges: 1) the poor conductivity of sulfur and its solid discharge products; 2) the notorious shuttle effect derived from the dissolution of long-chain polysulfides in organic electrolytes; 3) the huge volumetric fluctuation of element sulfur during redox reaction process [5–8].

Aiming to tackle these issues, tremendous efforts have been made by impregnating sulfur into conductive nanocarbon matrixes, including porous carbon [9,10], carbon sphere [11,12], carbon nanotubes and graphene [6,13–16], which promote the springing up of sulfur electrodes with high discharge capacities and improved cyclicity. However, it should be mentioned that the consumption of expensive precursor and tedious process result in economic and ecologic disadvantages, restraining the development of these carbon materials. Recently, given the wide source, simplicity in manufacture, relatively high conductivity, high porosity, porous biochar which is derived from solid residue produced in biomass pyrolysis process, has drawn great attentions as promising sulfur host for Li-S batteries [17–28]. Moreover, it has been demonstrated that some elements contained in biomass are capable of being diversely distributed in the products of biomass pyrolysis processes, conferring the possibility of in-situ heteroatomic doping in the carbon matrixes [29], which may significantly enhance the electric conductivity of the biomass carbonaceous host, together with improve the chemical adsorption ability to polysulfides [30]. Despite the overwhelming advantages, it is noteworthy that most of the reported biochar-based Li-S batteries are featured with a relatively low areal sulfur loading (ASL, < 2 mg cm−2) together with lower areal capacities (<2 mAh cm−2) [17–19,21–28], a representative energy density value for practical plug-in electrical vehicle design [31], which largely offsets the advantage in high performance Li-S batteries even although the sulfur percentage in many scenarios can easily get over 60 wt% [24,26,28]. These may be mainly ascribed to the following aspects: First, biochar particles, in most cases, possess irregular morphology and commonly have numerous crystal boundary, leading to higher charge-transfer resistance and longer mass transport path, which inevitably reduce active material utilization and rate performance [29]. Second, most reported biochar is undoped or mono-doped, and the contents of the dopants are very limited in carbon matrixes [25]. The weak chemical entrapment on polysulfides predominantly results in easy detachment of sulfur-related species from the host materials and thus causes capacity decay, especially when it comes to high-loaded electrodes [30]. Third, most reports did not refer to the chemical factor, namely noncatalyzed redox kinetics of polysulfides, which restricts the rate capability of Li-S batteries. That is to say, apart from chemisorption, a redox mediator or nanoelectrocatalyst aiming to propel redox kinetics of sulfur-related species and accommodate their deposition is seems more critical, while this is rarely reported in biochar-based Li-S batteries. Therefore, exploring a neoteric biochar host, which possess effectively structural and chemical dual-encapsulation of polysulfides, is highly demanded to develop high-performance Li-S batteries.

On the other hand, tobacco stem is one of the leftover materials from cigarette industry and tobacco agriculture [32]. Currently, over 95% of tobacco stem is disposed by incineration or landfills, resulting in grievous waste of resource and serious harm to human health and ecosystem [33]. Actually, the high carbon content of tobacco stem make it a suitable precursor for preparing biochar under fixed pyrolysis process [32]. Moreover, the rich presence of N, O, S elements grants it tridoped possibility in the biochar products [34]. Herein, we demonstrate a novel method to effectively trap polysulfides through the synergistic effect of textural constraint (physisorption), chemical obstruction (chemisorption) as well as acceleration in electrochemical redox reactions of polysulfides by using carbon nanodots-decorated alveolate N, O, S tridoped hierarchical porous carbon (a-NOSPC) derived from facile pyrolysis of tobacco stem. Taking advantages of such a design, the coexistence of micropores, mesopores, and macropore in the hierarchical porous carbon are beneficial for physical accommodating/immobilizing active materials sulfur and rapid charge/ion transfer. Moreover, the interpenetrating pore-in-pore structure in the alveolate framework not only alleviates volume expansion, but also provides adequate space for local Li2S deposition. Most importantly, the doped N, O, S atoms provide rich electrocatalytic active sites for chemical binding of polysulfides and thus expedited redox reactions of sulfur component, guaranteeing high utilization of active materials sulfur and improved rate capability. As a consequence, the a-NOSPC/S electrode with 72 wt % sulfur loading and ASL of 2.7 mg cm−2, delivers an unprecedented reversible capacity of 1074 mAh g−1 (2.90 mAh cm−2) at 0.1 C. Moreover, a capacity of 745 mAh g−1 (2.01 mAh cm−2) is maintained over 100 cycles with excellent Coulombic efficiency maintaining in the range of 96–99%. Our work offers a new strategy to simultaneously achieve high sulfur loading, large areal capacity, and long cycling life for Li-S batteries by developing the multifunctional biochar host material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of tobacco stem a-NOSPC biochar
ce:section.ce:para: A two-step strategy was adopted to prepare the tobacco stem-based biochar. Firstly, 5 g dried and smashed tobacco stem (collected from a local tobacco company, Changsha, Hunan) was dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water at room temperature under continuously stirring. The suspension solution was then transferred to a 100 mL steel autoclave and followed by heating at 200 °C for 5 h in a thermotank. The resulting hydrothermal product was filtered and washed repeated with the mixed solution of deionized water and ethanol. Finally, the as-prepared sample was dried at 80 °C overnight. The second step was related to high temperature carbonization process. Typically, the resulting products were mixed with a certain amount of KOH. The mixture was then placed in a tube furnace and carbonized at 700 °C for 2 h with N2 flow. Then, the resulting sample was adequately washed with alternant deionized water and ethanol. After vacuum filtration, the materials were dried at 80 °C for 12 h under vacuum.

The a-NOSPC/S composites were prepared by pressure-assistant melt-diffusion method. In a typical process, the a-NOSPC and sublimed sulfur (99.999%, Admas Company) with a precise weight proportion of 25:75 were blended together to form a homogenous mixture. The resulting mixture was then heated under N2 atmosphere at 155 °C for 12 h in sealed horizontal tube furnace with internal pressure of 105 kPa, followed by 220 °C for another 3 h. After cooled to room temperature, the a-NOSPC/S composites were obtained.

To form an uniform slurry, 75 wt% a-NOSPC/S composites, 15 wt% poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) and 10 wt% acetylene black were dispersed in N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), which was then homogeneously coated on an Al foil and dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 12 h. Subsequent cut into 12 mm disks with ASL of 1.1 and 2.7 mg cm−2 generated the a-NOSPC/S cathode. The CR2025-type coin-like cells were assembled with the above electrodes as the cathode and lithium foil as the anode. The electrolyte was 1 M Li bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl) (1,3-dioxolane/1,2-dimethoxyethane = 1:1, v/v) containing 1% LiNO3 and Celgard 2400 film was used as the separator. Galvanostatic charge/discharge measurement was conducted using Neware battery tester (Shenzhen, China) in the voltage range from 1.5 to 3.0 V. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were tested on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 604E). CV was carried out at a scanning rate of 0.05 mV s−1 within a voltage window of 1.5–3.0 V and EIS was measured in the frequency range of 100 kHz - 0.01 Hz.

The sample morphology was examined on a JSM-6700F scanning electron microscope (SEM, Japan) and G2F20 transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, TECNAI). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was conducted at room temperature on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer using monochromatic Al Kα as radiation source. The concentration of the polysulfide was evaluated by ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis, Shimazu UV-2550). X-ray diffractometer (RIGAKU- Miniflex II) was used to characterize the crystal structure of the samples. Raman spectra of the samples were measured on a Renishaw inVia system using 633 nm laser source. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed under N2 atmosphere on a STA449 C Jupiter thermo gravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH) at the heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The specific surface area of the samples were calculated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) equation according to the N2 adsorption-desorption curves obtained on the Micromeritics ASAP 2020 analyzer at 77 K.

The synthetic method of carbon nanodots-decorated a-NOSPC is schematically demonstrated in Fig. 1 . The typical morphological structures of the obtained biochar sample were observed by SEM and TEM. As depicted in Fig. 2 a–c, the resultant product shows a typical 3D honeycomb-like structure containing large amount of interconnected macropores with diameter ranging from 200 nm to 1 μm. The SEM image of the as-prepared a-NOSPC indicates that these macropores appear as a cross-link construction via thin carbon walls (Fig. 2b). Interestingly, plenty of endogenous cavities are visibly visualized in macropores, forming the interpenetrating pore-in-pore structure, which can help to the electrolyte penetration and facilitate the ion diffusion/transport. Most remarkably, abundant carbon nanodots with diameter less than 10 nm are decorated on the surface of a-NOSPC as revealed by the TEM image (Fig. 2d and e), giving rise to a heterodimentional structure. To our best knowledge, the 0D nanodots decorated 2D alveolate porous carbon derived from biomass materials is reported only rarely. Since the rich cellulose, hemicelluloses, and lignin in tobacco stem undergo complex and diverse pyrolysis mechanism [29], we speculate here that the asynchronized depolymerization, volatilization, and charring of individual moieties in tobacco stem may be the main factor that are assignable to the formation of this unique heterodimentional structure. It also should be mentioned that previous work have amply confirmed that rich oxygen functional groups located on the edge of the carbon nanodots are able to provide electrostatic interactions with sulfur [35,36], which are favorable for the uniform distribution of sulfur and the chemical entrapment of polysulfides. More closely, sp2 carbon domains with discernible interlayer spacing of about 0.40 nm can be detected in the short-range ordered region (Fig. 2f), larger than that of the pristine graphene, which can be ascribed to the doping of N, O, and S heteroatoms [37], which largely regulates the carbon lattices, as schematically shown in Fig. 1(IV). While no crystalline lattice arrangement are observed in the high-magnification TEM image (Fig. 2f), showing that the decorated carbon nanodots and carbon substrates are mainly amorphous, which is also supported by selected area electron diffraction (Fig. 2g). In addition, a number of surface defects (e.g., micropores and mesopores) on the surface of a-NOSPC can be observed as well (Fig. 2e). These can be attributed to the fact that the metallic K originated from potassium hydroxide can effectively intercalate into the carbon matrix during pyrolysis process, thus resulting in the generation of micropores/mesopores in the interior of carbon host [38]. The elemental mappings of C, O, N, and S on a-NOSPC composite further reveal that the doped elements of N, O, and S are successfully impregnated into the carbon matrixes (Fig. 2h-l).

The structure of the a-NOSPC was studied by XRD as shown in Fig. 3 a. Two broad peaks at approximately 24.5° and 43.5° are related to the (002) and (100) planes of carbon, respectively, which implies the amorphous a-NOSPC, in concert well with the TEM results (Fig. 2f and g). Also, the Raman spectrum of as-prepared a-NOSPC (Fig. 3b) depicts the two representative characteristic peaks at 1333.1 and 1602.4 cm−1, corresponding to the D (disordered carbon) and G (ordered graphitic carbon) band peaks of carbon matrix, respectively. The ID/IG ratio of 0.911 reveals the presence of crystalline defects and graphitization. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of a-NOSPC (Fig. 3c) can be identified as type I adsorption isotherm according to the IUPAC classification, implying the main existence of micropores. Pore size distribution pattern (Fig. 3d) demonstrates the hierarchical porosity of a-NOSPC with a wide pore size distribution from mesopores, to micropores and macropores, which in good agreement with the pore-in-pore structure observed in SEM results (Fig. 2b). Such an attractive hierarchical porous structure with high surface area of 833.2 m2 g−1 and pore volume of 0.65 cm3 g−1 grants a-NOSPC an ideally suitable reservoir for element sulfur and polysulfides, provides large accessible contact area and mutual interactions between the electrolyte and active substance sulfur, and endows rich channels for the electron/ion transfer and electrolyte penetration towards Li-S battery applications, as will be discussed further below.

XPS measurement was adopted to estimate the surface components of a-NOSPC material. The full XPS spectrum reveals that it is consisted of C, N, O, and S (Fig. 3e), corresponding element contents of 84.20 at%, 14.23 at %, 1.22 at %, and 0.35 at%, respectively. It is obviously that the peak of O is very high, indicating that tobacco stem raw material enriched in oxygen content cannot be thoroughly removed during pyrolysis process and the high-resolution XPS spectrum indicates that various O atoms remain in the a-NOSPC material by combining with various termination groups (carbonyl groups, hydroxyl groups, esters, carboxyl groups). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum listed in Fig. 3g can be divided into four different bands at about 284.4, 285.7, 286.9, and 288.9 eV regions, corresponding to CC, CO/CN, CS, and CO/CN bonds, respectively. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum of a-NOSPC indicates the presence of pyridinic N, pyrrolic, graphic, and oxidized-pyridinic N in (Fig. 3h). While the deconvolution of S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3i) reveals two main components of oxidized S (C-SOx-C) bond at 162.1–166 eV and thiophene-S (CSC) bond at 168–172 eV region, respectively [21,39]. It is worth noting that N and O incorporated into the carbon matrix, and the residual containing oxygen termination groups are capable of causing electrical charge redistribution and enhancing the chemical interaction between the a-NOSPC host and polysulfide guests via Lewis acid-base reaction, thereby efficiently prevent the dissolution of polysulfides [30]. Moreover, N and O codoping are considered to synergistically improve the electric conductivity, thus leading to an allowing of high discharge capacity [30]. Meanwhile, the chemical modification of S is also believed to further induce the formation of extra defects/disorders in the carbon matrixes, granting positive influence on attracting polysulfides and inhibiting their dissolution, as demonstrated by many groups elsewhere [21,40].

As our results verify the great potential in anchoring polysulfides of hierarchical porous structure and multiple heteroatomic doping, the interior pore-in-pore structure of a-NOSPC provides an ideally suitable reservoir for element sulfur that is both restricted in limited space and electrically accessible through the well-organized conductive framework. However, the prevalent melt-diffusion method may be less effective in impregnating element sulfur into carbon matrixes via single heat treatment, which leads to the exposure of abundant sulfur on the external surface of carbon [41]. Therefore, this versatile approach may be not applicable to the impregnation of sulfur into hierarchical porous and pore-in-pore structure due to the dominant presence of micropores in the a-NOSPC. We herein designed a pressure-assisted method that leverages the capillary effect in thermal-diffusion process to enable targeted impregnation of the a-NOSPC cavities with sublimated sulfur. With an increment of the heating up to 155 °C, the solid sulfur will transfer into liquid phase, whereupon the sulfur can be stably confine with the nanoporous of a-NOSPC via strong capillary forces [42]. Meanwhile, appropriate pressure-assistance at 220 °C can facilitate sulfur filling of a-NOSPC because the sulfur vapor will be apt to form liquid at certain lower pressure inside the nanopores of the materials [42,43]. SEM images display that the cavities of a-NOSPC host are efficiently filled with sulfur particles (Fig. 4 a). No bulk sulfur particles are detected on the external carbon surface, implying that most of sulfur confined within the internal pore-in-pore framework of a-NOSPC. The relevant element mapping suggests the relatively homogenous distribution of sulfur in the composite (Fig. 4b and c). Moreover, discernible nanosulfur quasi-microspheres appear at the surface of a-NOSPC with local enrichment at nanoscale observed in TEM image (Fig. 4d). This can be attributed to the partially rich loading of sulfur film on the external surface of decorated carbon nanodots, which may possibly be stemming from the affluent active groups grafted on the periphery of carbon nanodots [35,44]. This result further implies that the decorated carbon nanodots may provide extra adsorbing sites for polysulfides during cycling, thus cooperatively suppressing the leakage of active materials. XRD analysis was then performed to confirm the presence as well as the crystalline structure of sulfur inside a-NOSPC/S composite. As shown in Fig. 4e, it exhibits strong crystalline sulfur diffraction peaks (JCPDS No. 08–0247), implying the efficient loading of crystalline sulfur in the framework of a-NOSPC. Furthermore, the BET surface area is sharply reduced to 5.2 m2 g−1 (Fig. S1) after the incorporation of sulfur, indicating that sulfur filled into most of the pores. The final sulfur content in the a-NOSPC/S composite is calculated to be ∼72% according to the TGA plot (Fig. 4f).

To evaluate the electrochemical properties, CR2025 coin cells were assembled using the a-NOSPC/S as cathode and Li foil as anode. The electrolyte/sulfur ratio was about 15:1 (uL·mg−1). First, to enable a comparison between our results and previously reported biochar-based work, the a-NOSPC/S electrode with a medium ASL of 1.1 mg cm−2 was investigated via galvanostatic charge/discharge test [17–28], high-loaded electrode will be depicted in the next section. In addition, half-cells constructed with pure a-NOSPC present almost no obvious electrochemical reaction, with a minor capacity contribution of 3.4 mAh g−1 (Fig. S2). This result manifests that the as-prepared a-NOSPC makes a neglectable effect on the redox reaction during the charge/discharge process of a sulfur cathode. Fig. 5 a depicts the first five CV curves at 0.05 mV s−1. A cathodic peak locates at 2.34 V followed by a stronger one situated at 2.05 V corresponding to the stepwise formation of sulfur (S8) to long-chain polysulfides intermediates (Li2Sx, 4 ≤ x ≤ 8) and further to final solid products Li2S2/Li2S, respectively. When scanning back, a relatively wide peak at 2.44 V is assigned for the reversible conversion of Li2S2/Li2S back to polysulfides/elemental sulfur [4,5]. The obvious deviation of redox peaks can be attributed to the electrochemical activation of element sulfur within the interpenetrating pore-in-pore framework [4]. Impressively, the intensity of all peaks almost shows no shrinkage in the next 3–5 cycles, implying the fine cycle performance of electrode. The rate performance of the a-NOSPC/S was studied in the same voltage range as illustrated in Fig. 5b. At low current rate of 0.05 C, the a-NOSPC/S electrode delivers a high initial capacity of 1380 mAh g−1 and the discharge capacity stabilizes at 1340 mAh g−1 after continuous 5 cycles. Along with the increase of the current density, the a-NOSPC/S electrode delivers reversible capacities of 1225, 994, and 851 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.5, and 1 C, respectively. Though visible polarization and distortion emerge with increasing the current density (Fig. 5c), high reversible capacities of 648 and 447 mAh g−1 can be maintained stably even at medium charge/discharge rates of 2 and 3 C, respectively. Importantly, after changing the current rate back to 0.1 C, the a-NOSPC/S electrode is able to recover most of the original capacity (the 5th cycle at the former 0.1 C), implying the outstanding electrochemical stability of the a-NOSPC/S electrode.

The long-term cyclability of a-NOSPC/S composite electrode was checked at current rate of 0.5 C. As displayed in Fig. 5d, an initial reversible capacity of 1122 mAh g−1 is obtained, and this capacity slowly fades to 754 mAh g−1 after extensive 400 cycles with a low capacity decay rate of 0.082% per cycle. This prominent cycle stability also finds support from the EIS spectra of the fresh and cycled a-NOSPC/S electrodes. The experimental Nyquist plots are modeled based on the equivalent circuit (Fig. 5e and inset), in which the semi-circle is identified as the interface charge-transfer resistance (Rct) at the electrolyte-polysulfide interfaces. It can be seen that the Rct for a-NOSPC/S increases only slightly even after 400 cycles, indicating efficient immobilization of polysulfides during cycling [45]. It should be mentioned here that the recession of capacity may be more grievous when the charge/discharge rate was decreased or the content of sulfur was augmented, since a lower current rate leads to a longer retention time of the dissolved polysulfides in electrolyte, which may trigger greater shuttle effect and thus capacity decay [4]. Notwithstanding, as far as we know, the remarkable cycle stability at relatively low current rate of 0.5 C has been reported only rarely for biochar-based Li-S batteries with ASL over than 1 mg cm−2 so far [17–28]. The unique chemical and structural design of a-NOSPC host is regarded as the critical role during cycling: The hierarchical porous structure not only restrains the leakage of soluble polysulfide intermediates but also accommodates volume fluctuations of sulfur cathode as well as transfer Li+ ions in the electrolyte. Moreover, the decorated carbon nanodots provide extra adsorption sites for a-NOSPC to anchor polysulfides on the external surface, giving double protection for the stabilization of polysulfides. Meanwhile, the strong chemical entrapment for polysulfides rooted in the multiple hetroatomic N, O, S doping can be another critical factor that is responsible for the outstanding cycle stability. The multiple hetroatomic N, O, S doping permits the a-NOSPC host to adsorb polysulfides in a stable manner. The chemisorption is an intrinsic property between the polysulfides and a-NOSPC. As consequence, it will not decay severely during the long-term cycling test.

To further understand the strong chemical interactions between a-NOSPC host and polysulfide guests, the static-adsorption experiments and UV–vis spectroscopy were carried out. A representative polysulfide species Li2S4 (15 mM) was synthesized according to the literature [2,46], forming a transparent tawny solution. As listed in the inset in Fig. 6 a, obviously, the adding of a-NOSPC powder decolorizes the blank Li2S4 solution, implying either a strong adsorption capability of a-NOSPC or a chemical reaction between the two components. The UV–vis spectra indicate that the fresh polysulfide solution appears a strong absorbance at the 250–400 nm region (Fig. 6a), which is consistent with the existing literatures [47]. Remarkably, blank Li2S4 solution exposed to a-NOSPC exhibits drastically reduced absorbances in the 250–400 nm regions, consolidating the strong adsorption capability of a-NOSPC for polysulfides. These results vividly corroborate that the polysulfides can be effectively trapped within the a-NOSPC framework during cell operation.

To disclose the nature of this chemisorption on polysulfides, the surface chemistry of a-NOSPC after the static-adsorption experiment was subjected to examination by XPS analysis. The high-resolution Li 1s spectroscopy of pristine Li2S4 can be assigned to single Li-S component at 55.6 eV (Fig. 6b), which notably decreases to 55.2 eV after being adsorbed by a-NOSPC, which reveals the local electron transfer from the electron-rich heteroatom moieties to Li on carbon matrixes. With respect to high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. 6c), pristine Li2S4 exhibits two dominant constituents that are related to the terminal (ST-Li) and bridging (SB-SB) sulfur at 161.3 and 163.6 eV, respectively [48]. Very minor component at 167 eV is due to the sulfite formed in the synthesis process, which is commonly detected in stoichiometric Li2S4 or Li2S6 [48,49]. The amounts are, however, too minor to affect the overall chemical interactions tremendously. Similarly, the S 2p spectroscopy negatively shifts by more than 0.9 eV, with the ST-Li affected more than the SB-SB. These results manifest that the terminal S moiety captures more electronegative substance due to electrostatic dipole-dipole interactions [30,49]. Owing to the lone pair of electrons from N and O heteroatoms, the doped carbon matrixes could act as electron-rich donors, leading to the strong chemical binding with terminal Li ions in polysulfides by Lewis acid-base reaction [30,48]. Moreover, the doped N and O atoms that form π-conjugated structure are capable of accepting additional π electrons, thus further enhancing the dipole-dipole interactions [48]. Meanwhile, the S doping may favorably introduce defects or disorders on edge of carbon matrixes, which significantly enhanced the affinity of sulfur-related species to a-NOSPC carbon scaffolds [21,40]. It is of importance to note that these strong interfacial interactions between a-NOSPC and polysulfides may impose two main impacts on redox kinetics of polysulfides during cell operation. The primary avail is enhancing the immobilization of polysulfides and the associated one facilitates the charge transfer from doped-carbon matrixes to adsorb polysulfides and their redox conversion. In our case, benefiting from its high polarity, abundant ion-accessible sites, and high conductivity, a-NOSPC may provide easy access for charge to arrive at a-NOSPC/polysulfides nanointerface and to facilitate the polysulfide redox reactions. The redox kinetics of polysulfides was therefore studied with symmetrical cells, where the electrolyte contains 0.5 M Li2S6 and 1 M LiTFSI dissolved in mixture of 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME) and 1,3-dioxolane (DOL) (1:1, ratio by volume) (see details in supporting information).

The cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of symmetrical cells were first performed from the voltage range of −1 V to 1 V. As illustrated in Fig. 6d, the blank Li2S6 polarization profiles indicate that the current density is mainly provided by the redox reaction of Li2S6, while only a minor contribution is originated from the capacitive current as implied by the Li2S6-free symmetrical profile. Clearly, the redox current is greatly increased to about 4 times for the a-NOSPC-based symmetrical cells, demonstrating considerable improvement on the polysulfides redox kinetics (Li2S8 ↔ Li2S6 ↔ Li2S4). To highlight the efficiency of a-NOSPC, symmetrical cells were also separately fabricated based on some common carbon materials, including the commercial carbon nanotubes (CNTs), commercial conductive porous carbon black BP2000, and N-doped graphene (NG) (Figs. S3–5). It is obvious that the a-NOSPC presents larger redox current (Fig. S6), which strongly proves the a-NOSPC not only provides superb adsorption capability and abundant activation sites for polar polysulfides, but also effectively accelerates the redox kinetics of polysulfides. Moreover, drastically facilitated charge transfer of a-NOSPC is strongly testified by our EIS results in symmetrical cells (Fig. S7). The quasi-semicircle assignable to the Rct in the Nyquist plot of a-NOSPC is much smaller than those in CNTs, BP2000 and NG. Since the symmetrical cells are free of metallic Li, the lowest interfacial resistance is attributed to the enhanced interfacial affinity between a-NOSPC and polysulfides and rapid charge transport in the redox reactions. These results imply the tobacco stem-derived biochar possesses great potential to be used as efficient sulfur host for the practical Li-S batteries, in view of the overwhelming advantage in low cost as well as the unsophisticated fabrication process.

Considering the accelerated liquid phase conversion of polysulfides enabled by a-NOSPC, it is not a stretch to imagine that this liquid phase conversion may further affect the correlated liquid-solid conversion. In our case, the interpenetrating pore-in-pore structure of a-NOSPC provides large surface area and accessible sits for growth and deposition of the final solid species (S8 and Li2S2/Li2S) in the confined cavities. Concurrently, the a-NOSPC may alleviate the reaction impede from Li2S8 to Li2S6 and Li2S4, allowing these polysulfide species to attain dynamic equilibrium and to generate Li2S4-enriched zone around the N, O, S tridoped carbon substrates, which are favorable for subsequent Li2S nucleation and growth [2,5]. To ascertain the favorable deposition of final discharge product, the cycled a-NOSPC/S electrode in discharge state after 400 cycles at 0.5 C was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 6e and f, the alveolate structure is well maintained, with the increment of volume for the filled active materials (marked by red circle and arrows), owing to lower density of Li2S than element sulfur, indicating the interpenetrating pore-in-pore structure can efficiently buffer the volume change, thus inducing subsequent growth and precipitation of Li2S along the radial direction during cell operation. Moreover, affluent Li2S nanoparticles with diameters mainly ranging from 10 to 20 nm are detected in the joints of interconnected carbon walls as denoted in the magnified area of Fig. 6f, confirming the decorated carbon nanodots provide extra adsorption sites for the adsorbing of sulfur-related species via chemical bonding effect to decrease their leakage into the electrolyte. It's worth mentioning that three-quarters of the discharge capacities are theoretically contributed by the precipitation of Li2S from higher-order precursors (mainly Li2S4) [47]. The energetic evolution from higher-order precursors to Li2S ensures facilitated redox kinetics and high utilization of active materials in a-NOSPC/S electrode. As clearly verified, the cooperation between the engineered structure and heteroatomic doping induced chemical adsorption grant an optimal binding to polysulfdes and to catalyze the subsequent electrochemical reactions. As schematically illustrated in Fig. 1(IV), strong chemisorption on polysulfdes and abundant accessible sites in a-NOSPC allow to enriching the concentration of polysulfides on the conductive substrates. Meanwhile, the intimate contact between a-NOSPC and polysulfides promotes the electron transfer and expedites redox conversion of the sulfur-related species in the process of the electrochemistry reaction.

Achieving high ASL with corresponding high active material utilization is pivotal to high-areal specific capacity and high-volumetric energy density. The cycling performance of a-NOSPC/S with a high ASL of 2.7 mg cm−2 is listed in Fig. 7 a. Interestingly, the specific capacity of a-NOSPC/S electrode presents a gradual growth and achieves the maximum after 10 cycles at 0.2 C, manifesting that the activation reaction of interior sulfur may occur during prime charge/discharge course. Owing to such a high areal sulfur loading, the penetration of electrolyte into the whole electrodes will take some time. After the activation process, it delivers the peak discharge capacity of 1074 mAh g−1, equaling to 2.90 mAh cm−2, which surpasses the threshold value (2 mAh cm−2) of representative hybrid electric vehicles. And strikingly, a capacity of 745 mAh g−1 (2.01 mAh cm−2) is still preserved after 100 cycles with excellent Coulombic efficiency maintaining in the range of 96–99%. In addition, the charge/discharge profiles still display typical two-plateaus behaviour albeit with such a high areal sulfur loading (Fig. 7b), which re-conforms the facilitated electrode redox kinetics. The unique electrode architecture with high electric conductivity and strong chemisorption as well as facilitated conversion on polysulfides is predominantly responsible for the reversibility of this high-loaded electrode. The comparisons of the areal capacity in our reports with some recent biochar-based and porous carbon-based publications on Li-S batteries are shown in Fig. 7c. Actually, this tobacco stem derived bioachar-based electrode exhibits the highest areal capacity and long-life, making the a-NOSPC/S composites more fascinating than the other bioachar-based electrodes. To explore the promising energy density of this high loaded Li-S batteries, four a-NOSPC/S half-cells containing 12.2 mg of S were fabricated in series to achieve 9.31 V open-circuit potential. As illustrated in Fig. 7d, the Li-S battery series can intensively propel 60 yellow indicators of green 2835-type LED modules (the rated capacity and nominal voltage of the LED modules are 3 W and 12 V, respectively) without apparent brightness fade at least 30 min, making a-NOSPC/S cell promising in practical application.

In summary, a multifunctional host material of carbon nanodots decorated hierarchical porous carbon with interpenetrating pore-in-pore structure and N, O, S tridoping is synthesized via facile pyrolysis of tobacco stem, which is very efficient to entrap polysulfides by the synergistic effect of structural confinement and chemical barrier in a Li-S battery, leading to dramatically enhanced electrochemical performance. The a-NOSPC/S cathode with a high sulfur content of 72 wt% and ASL of 2.7 mg cm−2, exhibits high areal capacity of 2.90 mAh cm−2 at 0.2 C and maintains 2.01 mAh cm−2 after 100 cycles. The redox kinetic analysis on the fresh and postmortem electrode interface certify that the tridoped biochar has remarkable adsorption capability and electrocatalytic activity on polysulfide species. This study thus shed light on that the a-NOSPC/S electrode has fascinating potential for use in practical Li-S batteries.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financial supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21808053, 51703061, 21600191), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2018JJ3214, 2017JJ3103, 2018JJ3215), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2016M592427) and Hunan Agricultural University Foundation (SCX1801).
